text: <par>Lahu <latinform>cú</latinform>, despite its non-checked tone synchronically, reflects a PLB prototype with final stop, <reconstruction>*ʔjup</reconstruction>; the high-rising tone is the result of "glottal dissimilation", which occurred in pre-Lahu syllables that had both a *glottalized initial and a *final stop (which was reduced to <latinform>-ʔ</latinform> in Lahu). For a detailed account of this phenomenon, see Matisoff 1970, 1972a #73b.</par>
<par>HPTB (p.351, note i): "TSR has Lahu <latinform>jîʔ</latinform>, but DL:572 correctly gives <latinform>jêʔ</latinform> as the basic form."</par>

Lahu pɨ̄ ‘comb’ was assigned to PLB *ùl ʔkʷiy² in JAM’ s note to PKB’ s “Four forays into Karen linguistic history” (LTBA 5.1, p.27), by analogy with two other labiovelar roots, DOG (PLB ùl *kwiy²>WB ùl khwê, Mpi ùl khɯ², Lh. ùl phî̃) and NEST (PLB *kwiy¹ (e1611)>Mpi ùl khɯ⁶, Lh. ùl phĩ) [see JAM 1978 MLBM p.6]. However, it is probably better to group Lahu ùl pĩ with WB pùl hî~ùl phrî (<PLB ùl *ʔb(r)i(y)²).
[WHERE ARE THESE SO-CALLED LAHU FORMS FROM??]
<par>The high rising tone in Lahu indicates a stopped final, so that the form should be assigned to the <latinform>s(y)ok</latinform> allofam.</par>
<par>The last syllable (<latinform>nɛ̂ʔ)</latinform> probably means ‘wet’ (cf. modern computer slang ‘wet-ware’). </par>
<par>The last syllable (<latinform>nɛ̂ʔ</latinform>) probably means 'wet'; cf. modern computer-nerd slang 'wet-ware.'</par>
